text:
Powered byOutside

Van Aert, shown here winning silver at the 2023 worlds, is a medal favorite.(Photo: DAVID PINTENS/BELGA MAG/AFP via Getty Images)

PARIS (Velo) — FromRemco EvenepoeltoChloé Dygert, andFilippo GannatoGrace Brown, there are a lot of big names for gold in the individual time trial at theParis Olympic Games.



What the race of truth doesn’t pack is clear five-star favorites.

The time trial opensroad racing in the Paris Gameson Saturday, putting the individual race against time center stage as organizers slotted the road race — which typically opens Olympic competition — next weekend instead.

Neither of the defending champions — Primož Roglič nor Annemiek van Vleuten — will be racing, so the gold medals are up for grabs.

On the women’s side, the race is wide open, with a mix of riders hoping to peak just in time for Olympic glory.

The big question mark for the elite men’s race is which type of rider will be favored? Those who arecoming off the Tour de France, which concluded Sunday in Nice, or those who trained exclusively for the TT without the stress and pressures of racing?

Another question for the men’s field will be who the relatively short distance at 32.4km course — on par with what the women race — will favor? Will the big-engine TT specialists like Ganna, who typically dominates on courses topping 50km, be at a disadvantage on the shorter, more explosive distance?

It should make for some exciting racing set against a spectacular course in central Paris. Let’s dive in:

Americans Dygert andTaylor Knibbboth start with medal ambitions.

Dygert is a two-time world champion also intent on making up for an underperformance in Tokyo when she was seventh after coming off a horrific injury in 2020.

Knibb, who will also compete in triathlon as a rare two-sport Olympian in Paris, could be a medal outsider.

The Dutch bring medal contenders inDemi Vollering, who is arguably the best racer in the world right now, and Ellen Van Dijk, who is battling back from injury this spring after she returned to the peloton following a maternity leave.

“I haven’t ridden that many time trials in my life, and for sure one so flat. Normally that’s not necessarily something that suits me very well,” Vollering toldWielerflits. “When the distance is a bit longer it favors me more, so I do not know what to expect.”

Top 5️⃣ Contenders for the Women’s Individual Time Trial at the@Olympics🙌Get ready for an epic showdown in the at@Paris2024where the riders will fight for the fastest time⏱️Who do you think will come out on top? 💪#Cycling#OlympicCycling#RoadCyclingpic.twitter.com/1CH2QkneQ2



Elisa Longo Borghini of Italy, who crushed the Giro d’Italia Women in July, is another big favorite. She’s won two time trials this season, and should be in top condition.

Grace Brownof Australia, winner of Liège-Bastogne-Liège Femmes this spring, says anything less than a medal will be disappointment.

Lotte Kopeckyof Belgium, who’s been the world No. 1 all season, is a favorite in any race she starts despite admitting the was infected with COVID-19 in her pre-Games preparation.

“I’m not really afraid of COVID, because I came out of the Giro an infection. I’m immune to it for a while, so I don’t think it will impact my performance,” Kopecky said. “I took it easy for a few days, but last weekend on the track everything felt good. I hope it will not have any impact.”

When pure power counts, the reigning world champion is a threat in any terrain.

A total of 69 riders – 34 men and 35 women – will contest for the medals. Ethan Hayter is a late-hour non-starter for Team GB.

The list of favorites is long, but a few obvious candidates stand out.

The men’s TT could see a duel between specialists Ganna of Italy and rising TT starJosh Tarling. The British rider, the reigning European TT champ, won the national title in June as well as took a win at Gran Camiño, but finished behind Evenepoel at the Critérium du Dauphiné in June.

“I’d like to win it, but there’s no expectation. As long as I know I’ve done everything I can on my side, I’ll be happy with that,” Tarling said a media call. “Others have done more than me [in time trials], so I am a bit under the radar.”

Ganna, a two-time world TT champion, beat Tadej Pogačar in a similarly distanced time trial at the Giro d’Italia in May. With Ganna also racing the track to defend Italy’s gold in team pursuit, the Italian wants to make up for a disappointed fifth in Tokyo with at least a medal.

Neither raced the Tour and will race Saturday with fresher legs. Other candidates whodid not race in July at the Tourinclude AmericansBrandon McNultyandMagnus Sheffield, Denmark’s Mattias Skjelmose, and Norway’s Tobias Foss.

Top 5️⃣ Contenders for the Men’s Individual Time Trial at the@Olympics🙌The stage is set for an epic showdown at@Paris2024and we can’t wait to see the riders battle it out for the fastest time ⏱️Who do you think will claim victory? 🔥#Cycling#OlympicCycling#RoadCyclingpic.twitter.com/6zN7ntUnaM



Riders coming off the Tour could be a bit more gassed, but will have the advantage of three weeks of intense racing in their legs, perhaps giving them more staying power and reserves.

Wout van Aert, who testeddouble disk wheels during training, said the Tour helped him sharpen his form after coming back from injury in March.

“I feel good. The Tour is in my legs, but I am confident that I will be good on Saturday,” Van Aert toldSporza. “I definitely needed the Tour to take a step forward.”

All eyes will be Evenepoel, hot off winning one of the Tour’s two time trials en route to finishing third overall and claiming the white jersey in what was an otherwise spectacular Tour debut for the Belgian star.

The reigning world time trial champion Evenepoel downplayed his chances after the effort to reach the Tour podium.

“On Monday I spent most of my time in bed, on Tuesday I cycled a bit, but that didn’t feel great,” Evenepoel said at a press conference on Thursday. “I have to wait and see how things go on Saturday. It was a demanding final week in the Tour. Recovery isn’t easy, but I still have two days. We’ll see if it will be enough. I certainly came out of the Tour full of confidence and that already gives me a mental advantage.”

Other riders coming off the Tour include Belgium’s Wout van Aert, Swiss rider Stefan Küng, Canada’s Derek Gee, and Germany’s Nils Politt.

The setting for theOlympic time trial coursewill be the backdrop of Paris, so it should be a feast for the eyes.

For the first time in Olympic history, the women and men will race on the same course over the same distance at 32.4km.

The races start and finish along the River Seine, with the starts at Invalides and the finish line on the dazzling golden Pont Alexandre III.

The course hugs the south bank – orrive gauche– as it drives east toward the Bois de Vincennes. The route sweeps past Notre-Dame twice in each direction on the out-and-back course.

Riders will sweep past such Paris icons as Saint-Germain-des-Prés, Place de la Bastille, Vélodrome Jacques Anquetil, Château de Vincennes, and Place de la Nation. The two time checkpoints are at kilometers 13.1 and 22.

The field of riders in each race will start at 90-second intervals. After looping past the Bastille, the finish line is right on top of the ornate-gold Pont Alexandre III, an ideal setting forthe next Olympic champion.

The urban course features most wide boulevards interlaced with some sweeping corners. Mostly flat with a total of 150m in elevation gain, there are no major technical challenges except for some sweeping corners in the Bois de Vincennes.

🇬🇧 The individual time trial race route of the Olympic Games Paris 2024 on an OpenStreetMap map and the start order and times for the ladies and the men#Olympics#Olympics2024#Paris2024#raceroute#timetrial#cycling#roadcyclinghttps://t.co/3mrXiBYtM1



The urban nature of the course has already drawn some criticism.

“You have to be alert all the time,” Kopecky said of the course. “There are a lot of holes, which makes it difficult to find a good line or to stay in position. The road surface is not great.”

The mostly straight closing 10km back toward the finish will let everyone open up their engines.

Weather could be a factor, especially for the later starters in the men’s race.

Forecasters are calling for a 70 percent chance of showers Saturday afternoon, with light northerly winds of 5 to 10kph.
title: Rain, urban course form challenging time trial for Olympic favorites
author: Andrew Hood
description: Here are the top favorites for the Olympic time trial, with the threat of rain and a choppy urban course creating unpredictable race.
